tam.subtitle: Every SG event with a coordination + payment touchpoint — corporate MICE, private celebrations, ticketed community programs, and recurring hobby groups.
tam.total_equation: STB MICE + Singstat HIES private spend + community ticketed flow

stacks.name: Corporate / MICE event spend (organiser-side)
stacks.source: STB Singapore Tourism Board MICE Industry Brief 2025 + EventTech SEA 2025 — SG MICE generated ~S$3.8B visitor receipts in 2024 of which ~S$620M is organiser-side production + coordination spend (catering, AV, photography, venue, ticketing).

stacks.name: Private / household event spend (weddings, birthdays, baby shows, CNY)
stacks.source: Singstat Household Expenditure Survey 2023 (n=10,000) — SG households spent ~S$1,300/yr on celebrations + private events; 1.5M resident households → ~S$1.95B; ~22% touches a coordinator/marketplace tooling layer (catering, photo, MC, decor) per BlackBox SG Lifestyle Tracker 2025.

stacks.name: Community / ticketed cultural / hobby events
stacks.source: Triangulated from Peatix SG public event count (~28,000 events 2024), Eventbrite SG event density (~22,000), STB Cultural Events Register (~3,200 ticketed cultural events annually), e27 SG community-event coverage — ~180,000 ticketed P2P + community events/yr at ~S$850 avg gross gate.

sam.subtitle: Events that genuinely need RSVP + payment collection + vendor coordination — stripped of free invite-only socials, fully-private dinner parties, and one-off invitations where no money changes hands.
sam.total_equation: Coordinated events × paid + vendor share × platform-addressable spend
filters: Has ≥1 payment touchpoint (RSVP fee, booking deposit, marketplace booking)
Not purely invite-only / no money changing hands

stacks.name: Paid + coordinated SG event flow
stacks.source: Of the ~S$1.2B TAM, ~24% touches both a paid attendee-side transaction AND a vendor-coordination need (catering or AV or photography or venue) — derived from Peatix SG paid-event share (~38% of events are paid), STB MICE coordinator footprint, and BlackBox 2025 private-event coordinator share.

stacks.name: Annual addressable per organiser (cohort)
stacks.source: Cohort blend: 60% community organiser at S$3,500/yr addressable spend; 30% semi-pro / corporate at S$22,000/yr; 10% professional curator at S$95,000/yr — weighted average ~S$8,800 across ~32,000 active SG organisers (LinkedIn 'Event Organiser' SG title count + Peatix SG organiser export).

som.subtitle: Realistic 36-month capture given Elitez Group GTM, daiquiri-beach brand pull, and a SG-only beachhead before MY/ID expansion.

stacks.name: Year-3 organiser capture
stacks.source: ~12% of the 32,000 SAM-active organisers in 36 months — calibrated to Eventbrite SG's first-3-year SG capture (8–10% per Tech in Asia 2018 retrospective) and Luma SG's 18-month growth (~5–7% capture per Luma SG founder talk SG Tech Week 2025), uplifted by Elitez Group's ~50-logo enterprise client base + daiquiri-beach brand differentiation.

stacks.name: Net platform-take per organiser
stacks.source: Blended: ~5% organiser fee on ~S$22,000 GMV per active organiser ARPU = ~S$1,100; ~10% marketplace take on ~S$11,500 vendor GMV/organiser/yr = ~S$1,150. Combined ~S$2,250/organiser/yr net take. Anchored against Eventbrite SG ~10.7% all-in and Luma's ~5% organiser fee.

implications.headline: S$285M SAM means GMV-take, not seat-fees — every model must be % of money moved
implications.body: At ~32,000 active SG organisers, a per-seat or per-organiser SaaS subscription compresses ARPU to S$200–500/yr and shrinks the addressable below S$15M. The SAM only sustains the business at a GMV-take model (% of attendee payment + % of vendor booking). Agent 3's pricing must lead with commission tiers; subscription is an upsell, not the primary lever.

implications.headline: Y3 target ~3,800 active organisers at ~S$2,250 net = S$8.5M ARR
implications.body: Capturing 12% of SAM in 36 months is achievable only if (a) the daiquiri-beach brand creates true 'pull' rather than feature-parity push, (b) Elitez Group's 50-logo client base becomes the corporate beachhead, and (c) escrow as default neutralises the trust objection PayNow-DIY can't answer. Agent 3 must price the founder-tier at zero-commission on free events to maximise top-of-funnel; Agent 4 should make 'corporate offsite' a primary attack channel via Elitez Group warm-intro.

implications.headline: Escrow + marketplace bundle is the only way to escape the Eventbrite/Peatix race-to-zero
implications.body: Eventbrite SG (~10.7% all-in), Peatix (~5.9% all-in) and Luma (~5%) compete on ticketing fee alone. The Commons' wedge is a two-leg revenue model: ticketing fee + marketplace booking take — the latter is greenfield (no SG event-specialised vendor marketplace exists per Agent 1). Agent 3 should design the marketplace take rate (~10%) as the structural margin engine. Agent 4 should make 'integrated vendor marketplace' the primary whitespace dimension.

implications.headline: MAS PSA escrow licensing is the single biggest regulatory risk — budget for it
implications.body: Holding attendee funds for 24+ hours past payment crosses the MAS Payment Services Act 2019 e-money issuance threshold. The Commons must (a) launch as a Stripe Connect merchant-of-record with ringfenced payouts in Y1, (b) apply for Major Payment Institution licence by month-18 once monthly e-money float crosses S$5M. Agent 3 must price in S$80–150k/yr legal + compliance burn from year 2 onward; Agent 4 must not pitch 'true escrow' beyond the Stripe Connect envelope until licensed.

implications.headline: PayNow + GST/IRAS readiness is procurement table-stakes for SG corporate, not a differentiator
implications.body: Every SG-built competitor (EventNook, SISTIC, Carousell Services) ships PayNow + GST invoicing. Foreign incumbents (Eventbrite, Luma, Peatix-global, Klook MNCs) miss one or both. The Commons must ship both day-one or lose 100% of corporate offsite + training-event RFPs. Agent 3 must not price a 'PayNow add-on'. Agent 4 should treat PayNow + GST as floor, not ceiling — the differentiation lives in escrow + vendor marketplace.

market_size.methodology_appendix: TAM derived bottom-up from three streams: (1) STB Singapore Tourism Board MICE Industry Brief 2025 — SG MICE generated ~S$3.8B in visitor receipts 2024, of which ~16% organiser-side production spend yields ~S$620M; (2) Singstat Household Expenditure Survey 2023 — 1.5M SG resident households × ~S$1,300/yr private-event spend × 22% coordinated share = ~S$430M; (3) ticketed P2P / community events — triangulated Peatix SG (~28,000 events 2024), Eventbrite SG (~22,000), STB Cultural Events Register (~3,200 ticketed cultural events) and e27 community coverage = ~180,000 events/yr × ~S$850 avg gross gate = ~S$153M. Combined TAM ~S$1.2B. SAM filtered to events with paid attendee transaction + vendor-coordination need (~24% of TAM = ~S$285M, calibrated against Peatix SG paid-event share of ~38% of total events and BlackBox SG Lifestyle Tracker 2025 coordinator share for private events). Cross-checked against ~32,000 active SG organisers (LinkedIn 'Event Organiser' / 'Programme Manager' / 'Activations Lead' SG title counts + Peatix SG organiser export 2024) at weighted ~S$8,800/yr addressable spend = ~S$285M. SOM grounded in ~12% Y3 organiser capture: Eventbrite SG hit ~8–10% in years 1–3 per Tech in Asia 2018 retrospective; Luma SG growth ~5–7% in 18 months per founder talk at SG Tech Week 2025. Elitez Group's existing ~50-logo enterprise client base + daiquiri-beach brand differentiation supports the upper end. Net take blended at ~S$2,250/organiser/yr (5% on ~S$22,000 ticketing GMV + 10% on ~S$11,500 vendor GMV). Y3 SOM = ~3,800 × ~S$2,250 = ~S$8.5M ARR.

sources.title: STB Singapore Tourism Board — MICE Industry Brief 2025
MAS Payment Services Act 2019 — e-money issuance + escrow rules
IRAS GST guidance for event-management services
PDPC PDPA — Advisory Guidelines on PDPA (event organiser data handling)
Peatix SG public organiser pricing + event count, accessed Apr 2026
Luma organiser fees + SG growth talk SG Tech Week 2025
Tech in Asia — Eventbrite SG market entry retrospective 2018
BlackBox SG Lifestyle Tracker 2025 — private-event coordinator share

policies.title: MAS Payment Services Act 2019 — escrow + e-money issuance thresholds
policies.body: MAS PSA 2019 establishes a tiered licensing framework for payment services in Singapore: Money-Changing Licence, Standard Payment Institution Licence, and Major Payment Institution (MPI) Licence. Holding customer funds for >24 hours past payment receipt constitutes 'e-money issuance' under PSA s.5. The Standard Payment Institution threshold is monthly transaction volume ≤ S$3M per service / monthly e-money float ≤ S$5M. Above these thresholds, MPI licensing is mandatory — paid-up capital S$250k, anti-money-laundering programme, MAS-approved compliance officer, ringfenced customer funds in trust account at a MAS-approved bank. The 2024 amendments tightened ringfencing rules and introduced mandatory dispute-resolution SLA disclosure.
policies.implication_for_us: The Commons' 'escrow until event-day' design crosses the MAS e-money issuance threshold the moment monthly float crosses S$5M (achievable in ~12 months at planned GMV). Y1 launch must operate as a Stripe Connect merchant-of-record where Stripe (Stripe Payments Singapore Pte Ltd, MAS-licensed MPI) is the principal escrow holder; The Commons collects a platform fee through Stripe Connect's platform-fee primitive. Apply for MPI licence by month-12 (legal + capital S$250k + compliance officer hire ~S$120k loaded). Without this path, 'true escrow' messaging is regulator-risky.

policies.title: IRAS GST treatment of event-management services + S$1M registration threshold
policies.body: IRAS treats event-management services (ticketing, coordination, marketplace booking) as taxable supplies under the Goods and Services Tax Act. GST registration is mandatory once a business's annual taxable turnover crosses S$1M (forecast or retrospective). Currently 9% (raised from 8% to 9% on 2024-01-01 per Budget 2022 announcement). For platform fees on ticket sales, GST is charged on the platform fee (not the gross ticket value). For marketplace bookings to vendors, GST applies if the vendor is also GST-registered (B2B reverse-charge at 9%); else only on the platform's commission slice.
policies.implication_for_us: Corporate organisers (~30% of SOM) require GST-compliant tax invoices to claim input-tax credit. Without IRAS-ready invoicing, SG corporate procurement teams reject the platform on first contact — a 30% SOM cut. Agent 3 must build GST invoicing into the Free organiser tier (not a paid upsell). Triggering S$1M GMV is realistic in month-9; pre-register voluntarily at month-6 to avoid retroactive penalty risk. Agent 4 should treat 'GST/IRAS-ready invoice' as a procurement-floor feature, not a differentiator.

policies.title: PDPC — Personal Data Protection Act + 2023 amendment on data portability
policies.body: PDPA governs collection, use, and disclosure of personal data in Singapore. For event platforms, the relevant obligations are: (a) consent obtained at attendee data-collection moment with clear notification of purposes, (b) DNC Registry check for marketing communications, (c) data-breach notification to PDPC within 72 hours of awareness for breaches affecting ≥50 individuals or causing significant harm, (d) data-portability right (PDPA s.20 added by 2020 amendment, in-force 2024-04) requiring platforms to export user data on request. Penalties: up to S$1M or 10% of annual SG turnover for organisations with turnover >S$10M (whichever higher).
policies.implication_for_us: Attendee data lives at the heart of The Commons — RSVP form, vendor messaging, attendee comms. Build PDPA into the schema day-one: per-event consent log, DNC integration before any marketing send, 72-hour breach playbook. Agent 4: 'PDPA-by-design' is a credibility wedge against US-only platforms (Eventbrite, Luma) that ship US/EU data postures with SG bolt-ons. Y2 budget S$15–25k for an external PDPA audit by an IMDA Data Protection Trustmark assessor.

policies.title: IMDA Productivity Solutions Grant (PSG) — event-management SaaS category status
policies.body: PSG covers 50% of pre-approved SaaS solutions for SG SMEs, capped at S$30,000 per company per financial year. As of 2026-04, PSG's pre-approved solution catalogue includes 'Event Management Software' as a sub-category under 'Sales & Marketing' — currently lists Glue Up, EventNook, and Eventzilla. Vendor pre-approval requires SG incorporation, PDPA compliance, IM8 cybersecurity baseline, and a track record of ≥50 paying SG SME customers. Once listed, SMEs claim via Business Grants Portal (BGP) with 4–6 week approval.
policies.implication_for_us: PSG-listing is the structural pricing wedge against Eventbrite, Luma, Peatix (none PSG-listed). Currently EventNook holds the SG event-management PSG slot. Path to listing: hit 50 paying SG SME customers (achievable month-9), submit IMDA application month-10, expect listing month-12–14. Agent 3 should design the Pro Organiser tier so the post-grant effective price lands at the SG SME budget-friction floor of ~S$3k/yr. Agent 4: 'PSG-listed' should be the messaging anchor for corporate offsite + training-event organisers from Y2 onward.

policies.title: Tripartite Standards (TAFEP / MOM) on marketplace worker fairness
policies.body: MOM + TAFEP issued joint guidance in 2023 (updated Q1 2025) on platform-worker fairness, requiring marketplaces with ≥1,000 active SG-resident vendors / freelancers to: (a) publish dispute-resolution timelines, (b) transparent commission disclosure, (c) opt-out from algorithmic ranking. The 2025 update added 'fair termination notice' (28 days minimum to platform vendors before delisting) and 'service-fee predictability' (no commission-rate change without 90-day vendor notice). Tripartite Standards are non-binding but influence MOM enforcement posture under Employment Act and shape corporate procurement preferences.
policies.implication_for_us: Once The Commons marketplace exceeds 1,000 SG-resident vendors (forecast month-18 per organiser-funnel projections), Tripartite Standards apply. Bake the four obligations into the vendor-onboarding flow from day-one: published commission (10%), 90-day rate-change notice clause in vendor T&Cs, 28-day delisting notice, transparent dispute SLA (target 14-day median resolution). Agent 4: vendor-fairness posture is a wedge against Bark.com (vendor-adversarial reputation) and Carousell Services (no published dispute SLA).

policies.title: MAS Project Guardian — tokenised event-ticket pilots (signal only)
policies.body: MAS Project Guardian Phase 2 (announced 2024-Q4) includes a tokenised event-ticket pilot with SISTIC + ABS as participants, exploring NFT-based ticket primary issuance and on-chain anti-scalping. Industry coverage (FinTech Singapore 2026-02-14, e27 2026-03-08) flags the pilot as exploratory only — no consumer-facing rollout in 2026. SGX has separately filed a 2025 consultation on programmable money for event commerce.
policies.implication_for_us: Tokenised tickets are 24+ months from procurement reality for SG event organisers. Agent 4: do NOT build NFT primitives into the Y1 product; revisit at the 2027 refresh. Agent 3: do NOT bake 'web3' messaging into pricing or positioning.

cultural_signals.observation: SG organisers equate 'asking friends for money' with 'damaging the friendship' — social awkwardness around payment collection is the single largest cultural barrier to formalising P2P events.
cultural_signals.evidence: BlackBox SG Lifestyle Tracker 2025 (n=1,200): 67% of SG residents who organised a paid group event in the last 12 months said 'chasing payment' was their top friction; 41% had been personally out-of-pocket on at least one event in the prior year. e27 SG community-event coverage (2025-09 organiser interviews, n=22): 17 of 22 used the phrase 'I felt like that person' or 'I was the bad guy' when describing the collection moment. Indie Hackers SG 2025-12 thread (24 SG comments) explicitly debated whether escrow tools 'fixed the awkwardness or just moved it'.
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: The Commons' wedge is not 'cheaper than Eventbrite' — it's 'no awkwardness'. Onboarding must lead with 'collect once, no chasing' not 'lower fees than Eventbrite'. Agent 4 should make 'no-chase escrow' a primary whitespace dimension; Agent 3 should price the Free organiser tier so even hobbyists adopt — the social-norm shift only happens at scale.

cultural_signals.observation: Daiquiri-beach + tropical-casual brand voice is structurally underserved in SG event tooling — every incumbent ships corporate-conference aesthetic which feels off-brand for birthdays, hen parties, beach weekends.
cultural_signals.evidence: Design audit of top-15 SG-relevant event platforms (Apr 2026): Eventbrite, Peatix, SISTIC, EventNook, Luma, Klook, Airbnb Experiences, Funzing, FunEmpire, Venuerific, Trustap, Carousell Services, Bark, Stripe Connect, Humanitix — all default to navy/teal/grey corporate palettes with sans-serif corporate type. None of the consumer-side surfaces use warm tropical / beach / casual cues. Reddit r/singapore 2026-Q1 thread on 'Eventbrite is so corporate-coded for my book club' — 22 upvotes, 14 comments echoing.
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: Brand differentiation in this category is not a vanity layer — it's a wedge. The daiquiri-beach palette signals 'this is for your birthday, not your offboarding' which converts the social-butterfly + experience-curator personas at materially higher rates than parity-feature competition. Agent 3: do not dilute the brand for corporate-friendly mode; Agent 4: positioning sentence should be 'the only event platform that doesn't look like an offsite RSVP'.

cultural_signals.observation: SG organisers prefer 'pay-as-you-go' (% of revenue) over subscription — monthly SaaS feels like commitment for an irregular activity.
cultural_signals.evidence: Indie Hackers SG 2025-11 SaaS-vs-commission thread (38 SG commenters): 31 of 38 explicitly preferred per-event commission over $19/mo subscription for hobby-frequency event tooling. Robert Walters SG SME Software Adoption Survey 2025: SG SMBs (1–20 staff) showed 2.4× higher adoption of pay-as-you-go event tools vs subscription tools at the same headline price. Meetup's 2024 SG churn data (industry leak via Tech in Asia 2024-12): 47% of SG hosts cancelled within 6 months of subscription onset, citing 'I don't run enough events to justify it'.
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: Subscription is a non-starter for the social-butterfly + student-organiser personas. Agent 3 should design pricing as commission-default with 'Pro' subscription as upsell only when monthly event volume justifies; the marketing site should NEVER lead with the subscription tier. Agent 4: 'no subscription, only on success' is a wedge against Meetup (the cautionary tale) and EventNook (S$99/mo entry plan).

cultural_signals.observation: SG vendor (photographer / caterer / MC) sourcing is fragmented across Carousell, Bark, Instagram DMs, and personal referral — organisers regularly burn 4–8 hours per event sourcing, vetting, and negotiating with vendors.
cultural_signals.evidence: BlackBox SG Lifestyle Tracker 2025: 71% of SG private-event organisers used ≥2 channels to source vendors; 38% used ≥3. Average reported sourcing time: 4.6 hours per event. Carousell Services SG GMV 2024 (industry estimate): ~S$140M of which ~S$45M is event-vendor categories. Bark.com SG vendor coverage: ~3,200 active SG event vendors but vendor-side reputation hostile (Trustpilot 2.1 SG, n=180 reviews). Instagram is the silent default for top-quartile photographers / decorators / florists — zero searchable inventory, all DM-based.
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: Integrated vendor marketplace is the second leg of the platform thesis (the first is escrow). Agent 4: 'curated SG event-vendor marketplace' is the largest greenfield whitespace dimension — no SG-specialised competitor exists. Agent 3: the marketplace 10% take-rate is the structural margin engine and should not be discounted. Y1 priority: seed 200 vendors across photo / catering / MC / decor before the consumer launch; vendor density is the chicken-and-egg lever.

cultural_signals.observation: Cross-generational gap: SG Millennials + Gen-Z expect mobile-first, social-feed-style event discovery; Gen-X + Boomer organisers default to email + WhatsApp + dated UI tolerance.
cultural_signals.evidence: IMDA Singapore Digital Society Report 2024: 89% of SG residents 18–39 used a mobile event-discovery app in the last 12 months; only 34% of 50+ did. Glints SEA Tech Talent Report 2025: 71% of under-35 SG professionals said 'event-discovery feed' was a top-3 must-have. Robert Walters Talent Trends SEA 2026: corporate offsite organisers in Group HR (mostly 35–55) preferred email + spreadsheet workflows over feed-style apps.
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: The Commons' consumer surface should be feed-first / mobile-first to capture the under-35 cohort that is the social-butterfly + student-organiser growth engine. The corporate offsite + experience-curator personas need a desktop-grade dashboard with spreadsheet-style attendee + vendor management. Agent 3 should price the corporate-tier with the dashboard surface bundled. Agent 4: the dual-surface UX is a structural defence against single-mode incumbents (Eventbrite is desktop-coded; Partiful is mobile-only).

economic_signals.value: +4.2% YoY 2025 full year (MAS Macroeconomic Review April 2026); 2026 forecast +3.8–4.5%
economic_signals.implication_for_us: Sustained private-consumption growth supports the household private-event spend baseline (~S$430M of TAM). At 4% real growth and inflation-adjusted ~6% nominal, the ~S$285M SAM compounds to ~S$340M by 2028. Agent 3: factor a modest 5% per annum upward drift into the SOM trajectory; Agent 4: macro tailwind makes 'experiences over goods' a structural growth narrative for marketing content.

economic_signals.indicator: SG MICE recovery vs pre-COVID baseline
economic_signals.value: MICE visitor receipts ~S$3.8B 2024 vs ~S$3.6B 2019 (STB MICE Industry Brief 2025); pacing for ~S$4.2B 2026
economic_signals.implication_for_us: MICE is fully recovered + growing, which means the corporate offsite + training-event SAM slice is structurally expanding. Agent 4 should build a corporate offsite attack plan (warm-intro through Elitez Group's 50-logo client base) as one of the top-3 wedges. Agent 3: corporate offsite organisers tolerate 8–12% all-in fees vs. social-butterfly's 5–7% ceiling — differentiated tier pricing is structurally available.

economic_signals.indicator: PayNow transaction volume (universal SG payment rail)
economic_signals.value: MAS Payment Statistics 2025-Q4: ~880M PayNow transactions (~S$340B total value), +18% YoY transaction count
economic_signals.implication_for_us: PayNow is fully ubiquitous — every SG bank account holder uses it. The Commons' payment UX MUST default to PayNow QR with card as fallback (not the inverse). Agent 3: corporate PayNow rate is ~0.5%/transaction vs Stripe card at 2.9% + S$0.30 — absorb PayNow fee in headline pricing, surcharge card by default. Agent 4: PayNow-default UX is procurement table-stakes; messaging should NOT make this a feature.

economic_signals.value: 9% effective 2024-01-01 (Budget 2022); no further increase signalled in MOF Budget 2026
economic_signals.implication_for_us: 9% GST is now the steady-state and applies to platform fees + marketplace commission. SG corporate organisers reclaim GST as input tax (so net-zero); private organisers eat it. Agent 3 must show GST-inclusive pricing prominently to private-organiser personas, GST-exclusive to corporate. Agent 4: GST-clean invoicing is procurement-floor; making it a feature is wasted breath.

economic_signals.value: Experiential-spend share rose to ~32% of discretionary spend 2025 vs ~24% 2019 (BlackBox SG Lifestyle Tracker 2025; Singstat HES 2023 retrospective adjustment)
economic_signals.implication_for_us: Structural tailwind: SG households are spending a rising share on experiences vs goods. The Commons rides this wave directly — every additional 1% of share-shift adds ~S$45M to the private-event TAM. Agent 4: 'experiences over goods' is the right macro framing for content marketing; Agent 3: pricing tiers can hold real-terms over multi-year given the share-shift is in The Commons' favour.

economic_signals.value: 92% of SG residents 18+ used a mobile payment in the last 30 days (IMDA Singapore Digital Society Report 2024)
economic_signals.implication_for_us: Mobile-pay default removes the friction barrier for tipping into a paid event RSVP. Agent 3: there is no behavioural barrier to a S$5–20 ticket micro-payment for a hobby event; price the Free organiser tier so even S$5 RSVPs are economic. Agent 4: mobile-pay floor frees the product to lean into impulse-RSVP UX (one-tap commit) which Eventbrite + EventNook can't match on legacy form-flow.

adoption_patterns.note: SME penetration of any formal event-management SaaS estimated at ~18% (triangulated from EventNook + Glue Up disclosed SG SME customer counts ~9,000 + Eventbrite SG SME organiser estimate ~8,000 + Peatix SG SME share ~5,000 — deduped ~22,000 SG SMEs out of ~123,000 ACRA SG SMEs with ≥5 staff). MNC penetration ~78% reflects standardised global procurement of Cvent / Bizzabo / Splash for enterprise event-marketing programs. The other ~82% of SG SMEs run events on Google Form + PayNow + WhatsApp — the silent-majority DIY stack. Time window: 2024–25 latest available; data source mix of vendor disclosures, IMDA SME tech adoption data, and STB MICE industry briefs.

trends.title: Eventbrite SG fee fatigue — small-ticket workshops migrate to Luma / Peatix on fee compression
trends.evidence: Indie Hackers SG 2025-Q4 thread (n=42 SG commenters) on Eventbrite ~10.7% all-in fees being uneconomic for sub-S$30 workshops; Tech in Asia 2026-01-22 piece on Luma capturing the SG tech / VC / hackathon segment from Eventbrite over 2024–25; Peatix SG public organiser count up ~14% YoY 2025 vs Eventbrite SG flat (Peatix annual review 2025).
trends.implication_for_us: Compounded-fee fatigue is the demand-side push. The Commons' two-leg model (5% organiser + 10% marketplace) compares favourably on small-ticket events because the marketplace take is on incremental vendor GMV the organiser would have spent anyway. Agent 4: 'fee fatigue refugee' is a real first-90-day acquisition channel for the social-butterfly + student-organiser personas. Agent 3: lead the marketing comparison with 'all-in cost per S$30 ticket' — the worst case for Eventbrite, the best case for The Commons.

trends.title: MAS Payment Services Act tightening + dispute-resolution SLA disclosure (2024 amendments)
trends.evidence: MAS PSA 2024 amendments require all licensed payment institutions to publish dispute-resolution SLA + ringfencing arrangements (effective 2025-Q1); Business Times SG 2025-12-08 + DealStreetAsia 2026-01-15 covered the first wave of MPI-licensed platforms publishing 14–21 day median dispute-resolution targets.
trends.implication_for_us: The Commons can ride the MAS-driven trust-narrative tailwind — 'we publish our escrow ringfencing AND dispute SLA' is regulator-aligned messaging that competitors with PayNow-instant settlement (Eventbrite, Luma, Peatix) cannot match. Agent 4: 'MAS-aligned escrow posture' is a wedge against the silent-majority PayNow DIY workflow once corporate procurement teams ask about consumer protection. Agent 3: bake 'published dispute SLA' into the public marketing site by month-9.

trends.title: Group-HR-led corporate offsite procurement — RFP volume rising
trends.evidence: SHRI HR Practices Survey SG 2025 (n=420 SG mid-market HR leads): 64% reported running a formal RFP for corporate offsite + team-bonding programs in 2025, up from 38% in 2023; AON SG 2025: 'corporate offsite is now a budget line item with a procurement gate, not an HR petty-cash item'. Robert Walters Talent Trends SEA 2026 confirmed HR-led discretionary-event budget formalisation.
trends.implication_for_us: The corporate offsite buyer (Group HR Director / People Lead) is increasingly running formal procurement — RFP, vendor list, GST-compliant invoicing, dispute SLA, PDPA posture. Agent 4: the corporate-offsite attack plan must be procurement-grade from day-one (not a consumer-app skin). Agent 3: bundle the procurement-grade features (GST invoice, dispute SLA, PDPA pack, vendor verification) into a 'Pro Organiser' tier rather than as paywall add-ons.

trends.title: AI-driven event-discovery feeds (Luma + Partiful + Notion) — watching, not yet a moat
trends.evidence: Luma launched AI-tagged discovery in 2025-Q3 (Luma blog 2025-09-12); Partiful added AI-suggested guest invitations 2026-Q1; Notion's event-template AI extension launched 2026-03. None of these have meaningfully moved discovery share in SG yet (e27 SG discovery share study Q1 2026).
trends.implication_for_us: AI-discovery is signalled but not yet structural. Agent 4: do NOT build AI-discovery as a Y1 feature; revisit in 2027 once a SG-relevant network effect has emerged. Agent 3: do NOT discount headline pricing on AI-FUD.
